question: Does the B-001 connected group breach APS 221 large exposure requirements, and what action is required?
answer:
# APS 221 Large Exposure Assessment: B-001 Connected Group

## Preliminary Note on Scope

The regulatory sources provided establish the **framework for assessing APS 221 compliance**, however they do not contain specific exposure data for a counterparty identified as "B-001." The following analysis applies the precise regulatory requirements to any B-001 assessment, which must be conducted against actual exposure figures.

---

## Regulatory Framework: Key Thresholds

| Threshold | Requirement | Source |
|---|---|---|
| **≥10% of Tier 1 Capital** | Constitutes a "Large Exposure" — triggers full APS 221 obligations | Source 4 |
| **≥75% of applicable limit** | Report to Risk Committee within **5 business days** | Source 2 |
| **≥90% of applicable limit** | Formal Board notification within **3 business days** | Source 2 |
| **>100% of applicable limit** | **Breach confirmed** — APRA notification within **5 business days** | Source 1 |
| **25% of Tier 1 Capital** | Maximum permissible large exposure limit (single counterparty or connected group) | Sources 4 & 5 |

---

## Breach Determination Criteria

### Step 1 — Connected Group Identification
Per **Source 3**, the B-001 connected group must be identified by traversing **at least six degrees of relationship**, including:
- Guarantor networks
- Trust structure linkages
- Indirect counterparty connections

> *"An ADI relying solely on individual loan assessments without traversing the full relationship network is not compliant with APS 221 connected counterparty identification requirements."*
contexts:
APS 221 — Prudential Standard: Large Exposures — Breach Reporting and Remediation

An APS 221 breach occurs when the exposure to a single counterparty or connected group exceeds 100% of the applicable large exposure limit. An ADI that identifies a breach must: (a) notify APRA within 5 business days of identification; (b) provide a written explanation of how the breach occurred; (c) submit a remediation plan within 15 business days; and (d) implement the remediation plan within the timeline agreed with APRA. A connected group exposure of 129% of the applicable limit represents a significant breach requiring immediate APRA notification and emergency board review. Failure to identify and report a breach in a timely manner attracts additional regulatory enforcement action under the Banking Act 1959.

APS 221 — Prudential Standard: Large Exposures — Monitoring Thresholds and Board Notification

An ADI must implement a tiered monitoring framework for large exposures. Where the aggregate exposure to a connected group reaches 75% of the applicable large exposure limit, the matter must be reported to the ADI's Risk Committee within five business days. Where the aggregate exposure to a connected group reaches or exceeds 90% of the applicable large exposure limit, the ADI's Board must be formally notified within three business days. The notification must include: the identity of the connected group, total exposure amount, applicable limit, utilisation percentage, and a proposed management action plan. Failure to notify the Board at the 90% threshold constitutes a material compliance deficiency under APS 221.

APS 221 — Prudential Standard: Large Exposures — Six-Hop Relationship Traversal Requirement

APRA expects ADIs to implement systems capable of identifying connected counterparty relationships that extend beyond immediate contractual links. Connected party relationships can span multiple degrees of separation: a borrower's guarantor may be linked through trust structures to additional guarantors who support other borrowers. An ADI relying solely on individual loan assessments without traversing the full relationship network is not compliant with APS 221 connected counterparty identification requirements. Systems must be capable of traversing at least six degrees of relationship to identify all members of a connected counterparty group and aggregate their exposures accurately.

APS 221 — Prudential Standard: Large Exposures — Definition of Large Exposure

A large exposure is defined as the total aggregate exposure of an Authorised Deposit-taking Institution (ADI) to a single counterparty or group of connected counterparties that equals or exceeds 10% of the ADI's Tier 1 capital. An ADI must not have a large exposure that exceeds 25% of its Tier 1 capital to any single counterparty or group of connected counterparties without prior APRA approval. Large exposure limits apply to the gross credit exposure before the application of credit risk mitigants, including guarantees and collateral, unless those mitigants are explicitly recognised under APS 221.

APS 221 — Prudential Standard: Large Exposures — Large Exposure Limits — Single and Group

The large exposure limit for a single counterparty must not exceed 25% of the ADI's Tier 1 capital at any point in time. For groups of connected counterparties, the same 25% limit applies to the aggregate exposure across all members of the connected group. An ADI may set internal limits below the APRA maximum — internal limits do not alter the APRA reporting and notification obligations. Where an ADI's systems identify that a borrower's guarantor network creates a connected group exposure, both the single counterparty exposure and the total group exposure must be assessed against their respective limits simultaneously.
context_titles: APS221 — Breach Reporting and Remediation
APS221 — Monitoring Thresholds and Board Notification
APS221 — Six-Hop Relationship Traversal Requirement
APS221 — Definition of Large Exposure
APS221 — Large Exposure Limits — Single and Group
ground_truth: Yes. The connected group exposure reaches 92% of the APS 221 large exposure limit. APS 221 requires Board notification when aggregate exposure exceeds 90% of the large exposure limit. Board notification is required immediately.

question: Does B-001 breach the APRA February 2026 DTI limit, and what is the applicable limit?
answer:
## DTI Breach Assessment for B-001

### Applicable Limit

Under the **APRA DTI Limit Activation Notice — February 2026 (Activation of DTI Limit — Effective Date and Scope)**, the applicable DTI limit is **6.0**, effective from **1 February 2026**, applying to all ADIs regulated under the Banking Act 1959.

---

### Breach Determination

**Yes, B-001 is in breach of the APRA February 2026 DTI limit.**

Based on the regulatory sources, a borrower with a DTI ratio of **7.2** constitutes a **material breach** of the 6.0 limit, as explicitly stated in the **DTI Calculation Methodology** source:

> *"A DTI ratio of 7.2 constitutes a material breach of the February 2026 limit of 6.0."*

---

### Consequences of This Breach

The following obligations are triggered:

| Obligation | Requirement | Timeframe |
|---|---|---|
| **Documentation** | Document borrower identity, current DTI, origination date, income basis, and income changes | Within **5 business days** of identification |
| **APRA Notification** | Report via ARF 223, including affected borrowers, aggregate loan value, DTI distribution, and remediation timeline | Within **5 business days** of identification |
| **Monitoring Plan** | Implement quarterly DTI reassessment and escalation triggers | Within **15 business days** of identification |
| **Remediation Plan** | Submit formal remediation plan to APRA | Within **30 business days** of identification |

---

### Key Risk Note

Failure to report an identified DTI of
contexts:
APRA DTI Limit Activation Notice — February 2026 — Reporting Requirements — APRA Notification

ADIs must report all identified DTI breaches to APRA within the standard Authorised Deposit-taking Institution Prudential Reporting Form ARF 223. Where a borrower DTI breach is identified after the 1 February 2026 activation date, the ADI must notify APRA within 5 business days of identification. The notification must include: the number of affected borrowers, the aggregate loan value, the distribution of DTI ratios, and the proposed remediation timeline. An ADI that identifies a borrower with a DTI ratio of 7.2 as of the activation date and has not yet reported this to APRA is in breach of its reporting obligations under the February 2026 DTI Limit Activation Notice. Unreported breaches attract additional regulatory scrutiny under the supervisory framework.

APRA DTI Limit Activation Notice — February 2026 — DTI Calculation Methodology

Debt-to-income ratio is calculated by dividing the borrower's total committed debt obligations by their gross annual income. Total committed debt includes all outstanding loan balances, including home loans, investment property loans, personal loans, and credit card limits at 3.8% of the credit limit. Gross annual income includes salary, wages, and verified rental income. Income from a single contract employer is included only to the extent the contract term extends beyond 12 months from the assessment date. Where the income contract expires within 6 months of the assessment date, the ADI must calculate an effective post-contract DTI using the borrower's expected income after contract expiry. A DTI ratio of 7.2 constitutes a material breach of the February 2026 limit of 6.0.

APRA DTI Limit Activation Notice — February 2026 — Existing Portfolio — Identification and Documentation

ADIs are required to identify all existing loans in their portfolio where the current borrower DTI exceeds 6.0. These pre-existing breaches must be documented within 5 business days of identification. Documentation must include: the borrower's identity, the current DTI ratio, the date the loan was originated, the income basis used at origination, and any changes in income since origination that have affected the DTI. An ADI that identifies a borrower with a DTI of 7.2 must document this within 5 business days and report the identification to APRA through the standard prudential reporting framework. Failure to identify and document existing breaches within the required timeframe is a separate compliance obligation from the reporting of the breach itself.

APRA DTI Limit Activation Notice — February 2026 — Remediation Requirements and Timeline

Where an ADI identifies existing loans with DTI ratios above 6.0, the ADI must implement a monitoring plan within 15 business days of identification. The monitoring plan must include: quarterly DTI reassessment, income verification at each renewal or variation, and escalation triggers for DTI increases above 8.0. For borrowers with DTI ratios above 8.0 or approaching 9.0 due to income expiry, the ADI must implement active remediation including review of the loan structure and assessment of the need for additional security or guarantor support. APRA may require the ADI to reduce the DTI through additional repayments, loan restructuring, or the application of additional security. Remediation plans must be submitted to APRA within 30 business days of the identification of a material breach.

APRA DTI Limit Activation Notice — February 2026 — Activation of DTI Limit — Effective Date and Scope

The Australian Prudential Regulation Authority (APRA) hereby activates restrictions on new residential mortgage lending where the borrower's debt-to-income (DTI) ratio exceeds 6.0. This restriction takes effect from 1 February 2026 and applies to all Authorised Deposit-taking Institutions (ADIs) regulated under the Banking Act 1959. The DTI limit of 6.0 applies to all new residential mortgage originations from the activation date. An ADI must not extend new residential mortgage credit to any borrower whose DTI ratio would exceed 6.0 as a result of the new lending.

DTI_LIMIT_FEB2026 — DTI Calculation Methodology

DTI_LIMIT_FEB2026 — Remediation Requirements and Timeline
DTI_LIMIT_FEB2026 — Activation of DTI Limit — Effective Date and Scope
ground_truth: Yes. B-001 has a DTI ratio of 7.2. The APRA February 2026 DTI Limit Activation Notice sets a limit of 6.0 for new lending. A DTI of 7.2 exceeds the limit of 6.0.

question: What are the reporting requirements for an identified DTI breach under the February 2026 APRA notice?
answer:
## Reporting Requirements for Identified DTI Breaches — February 2026 APRA Notice

Based on the regulatory sources provided, the following requirements apply:

---

### 1. Reporting Instrument
DTI breaches must be reported to APRA via the **Authorised Deposit-taking Institution Prudential Reporting Form ARF 223**. *(Source 1: DTI_LIMIT_FEB2026 — Reporting Requirements)*

---

### 2. Notification Timeframe
Where a borrower DTI breach is identified **after the 1 February 2026 activation date**, the ADI must notify APRA **within 5 business days of identification**. *(Source 1)*

---

### 3. Required Notification Content
The notification must include all four of the following elements:
- The **number of affected borrowers**
- The **aggregate loan value**
- The **distribution of DTI ratios**
- The **proposed remediation timeline**

*(Source 1: DTI_LIMIT_FEB2026 — Reporting Requirements)*

---

### 4. Consequences of Non-Reporting
An ADI that fails to report an identified breach — for example, a borrower with a DTI ratio of **7.2** — is in **breach of its reporting obligations** and will attract **additional regulatory scrutiny** under the supervisory framework. *(Source 1)*

---

### 5. Important Distinction
The reporting obligation is **separate** from the documentation obligation. Documentation of identified breaches must occur **within 5 business days of identification** and constitutes a distinct compliance requirement. *(Source 2: DTI_LIMIT_FEB2026 — Existing Portfolio — Identification and Documentation)*

question: Does the RETAIL_PROP sector concentration require escalation under credit policy, and what is the escalation requirement?
answer:
## RETAIL_PROP Sector Concentration — Escalation Analysis

### Current Position

Based on the regulatory sources, the RETAIL_PROP sector has a combined exposure of **AUD 12.4 million**, representing **78% utilisation** of the approved **AUD 16 million** sector limit (Source 3).

---

### Escalation Requirements Triggered

#### 1. Mandatory Monitoring Report — 75% Trigger (§7.3.1)
The 78% utilisation **exceeds the 75% threshold**, triggering the following mandatory requirements under Credit Policy §7.3.1 (Source 1):

- The **Credit Risk team must prepare a formal Sector Concentration Monitoring Report**
- The report must be **submitted to the Risk Committee within 10 business days**
- The report must include:
  - Total current exposure
  - Number of borrowers in the sector
  - Top five individual exposures
  - Growth rate over the preceding 12 months
  - Sector economic outlook
  - Connected party relationships linking borrowers within the sector
- The **Risk Committee must acknowledge receipt within five business days**

#### 2. Connected Guarantor Network Escalation (§7.3.3)
**Independently of the utilisation percentage**, a separate escalation obligation is triggered under Credit Policy §7.3.3 (Source 2):

- Borrowers **B-001, B-002, B-003, and B-007** are all classified as **SECTOR_CODE = RETAIL_PROP** and are connected through a **common guarantor network**
- Credit Policy §7.3.3 explicitly requires that **any connected guarantor network spanning four or more borrowers in the same sector must be reported to the Risk Committee regardless of
contexts:
Internal Credit Policy — §7.3 Sector Concentration Limits and Escalation — Monitoring Thresholds — 75% Trigger

Where the aggregate portfolio exposure to any single sector reaches or exceeds 75% of the approved sector concentration limit, the Credit Risk team must prepare a formal Sector Concentration Monitoring Report and submit it to the Risk Committee within 10 business days. The monitoring report must include: total current exposure, number of borrowers in the sector, the top five individual exposures, growth rate over the preceding 12 months, sector economic outlook, and any connected party relationships that link borrowers within the sector. A combined RETAIL_PROP exposure of AUD 12.4 million at 78% of the AUD 16 million sector limit triggers the monitoring report requirement under Credit Policy §7.3.1. The Risk Committee must acknowledge receipt of the report within five business days.

Internal Credit Policy — §7.3 Sector Concentration Limits and Escalation — Connected Borrower Aggregation for Sector Monitoring

Where multiple borrowers are connected through common guarantors, shared ownership structures, or family trust arrangements, and all fall within the same industry sector, their combined exposure must be aggregated for sector concentration monitoring purposes. A guarantor network connecting four borrowers — B-001, B-002, B-003, and B-007 — all of whom have SECTOR_CODE = RETAIL_PROP, creates a systemic sector concentration that exceeds what individual loan analysis reveals. The systemic risk of four connected borrowers in the same sector, exposed through the same guarantor network, is qualitatively different from four unconnected borrowers in the same sector and must be escalated separately even if the sector limit has not been formally reached. Credit Policy §7.3.3 requires that any connected guarantor network spanning four or more borrowers in the same sector is reported to the Risk Committee regardless of utilisation percentage.

Internal Credit Policy — §7.3 Sector Concentration Limits and Escalation — Sector Concentration Limits — RETAIL_PROP

The approved sector concentration limit for the RETAIL_PROP (Retail Property) sector is AUD 16 million. This limit represents the maximum aggregate portfolio exposure to borrowers whose primary loan purpose is the acquisition, development, or refinancing of retail property assets. For the purposes of this section, aggregate portfolio exposure includes: direct loans to retail property borrowers, guarantor-supported exposures where the guaranteed loans relate to retail property, and any off-balance-sheet commitments. A combined RETAIL_PROP sector exposure of AUD 12.4 million represents 78% utilisation of the AUD 16 million sector limit. All borrowers classified with SECTOR_CODE = RETAIL_PROP in the BCA_SECTOR system records are included in this calculation.

Internal Credit Policy — §7.3 Sector Concentration Limits and Escalation — Sector Concentration — Regulatory Reporting Obligations

Sector concentration monitoring is required not only by internal credit policy but also by APRA's Prudential Practice Guide CPG 220 on credit risk management. Where APRA requests evidence of sector concentration monitoring, the ADI must be able to produce: the sector concentration limits approved by the Credit Risk Committee, current utilisation reports by sector, the escalation history for any sector that has exceeded 75% utilisation, and evidence that connected borrower aggregation has been applied correctly. An ADI that monitors sector concentration at the individual borrower level without aggregating connected party exposures is not meeting the spirit of CPG 220 sector monitoring requirements. The internal sector concentration system must capture SECTOR_CODE from BCA_SECTOR records and aggregate across all connected parties identified through BUT050 and BCA_GUARANTOR relationship mapping.

Internal Credit Policy — §7.3 Sector Concentration Limits and Escalation — Escalation Threshold — 90% Credit Committee Review

Where the aggregate portfolio exposure to any single sector reaches or exceeds 90% of the approved sector concentration limit, the matter must be escalated to the Credit Committee for formal review. The Credit Committee must convene within 15 business days of the threshold being reached and must consider: whether to approve continued lending in the sector, whether existing exposures should be reduced through active management, and whether the sector limit itself should be revised. At 90% utilisation the Credit Committee has the authority to impose a lending moratorium on new sector originations pending a full portfolio review. Failure to escalate to the Credit Committee when the 90% threshold is reached is a material breach of Credit Policy §7.3.

CREDIT_POLICY — Connected Borrower Aggregation for Sector Monitoring
CREDIT_POLICY — Sector Concentration Limits — RETAIL_PROP
CREDIT_POLICY — Sector Concentration — Regulatory Reporting Obligations
CREDIT_POLICY — Escalation Threshold — 90% Credit Committee Review
ground_truth: Yes. A combined RETAIL_PROP exposure of AUD 12.4 million represents 78% of the AUD 16 million sector limit. Credit Policy §7.3.1 requires a formal Sector Concentration Monitoring Report to the Risk Committee when exposure exceeds 75% of the sector limit. A monitoring report must be submitted.

question: Which APRA standards are relevant to B-001 complete risk profile?
answer:
## APRA Standards Relevant to a Complete Risk Profile for a Borrower (B-001)

Based on the provided regulatory sources, the following APRA standards are directly relevant to constructing and maintaining a complete risk profile:

---

### 1. **CPS 230 — Operational Risk Management (AI and Automated Decision Systems)**
*(Sources 1 & 2)*

Any AI or automated system used to generate or assess B-001's risk profile must ensure:
- Outputs are **explainable and auditable**
- Every material recommendation is accompanied by an **evidence trail linking it to underlying data**
- **Human oversight is embedded architecturally** — not merely optional
- The system cannot make **autonomous decisions** that would normally require human authorisation
- CPS 230 applies to all material operational processes including **credit risk assessment** — effective **1 July 2025**

---

### 2. **CPS 230 — Audit Trail Requirements for AI Decisions**
*(Source 5)*

A complete risk profile generated with AI assistance must include:
- **(a)** Specific data records consulted
- **(b)** Reasoning steps taken
- **(c)** Confidence levels assigned to each finding
- **(d)** Acknowledged data gaps or uncertainties
- **(e)** Identity of the reviewing human officer
- **(f)** Timestamps of both AI recommendation and human approval

> ⚠️ An AI system that generates findings **without citing specific TRBK table records** does not meet CPS 230 audit trail requirements.

---

### 3. **CPG 220 — Credit Risk Management**
*(Source 3)*

For B-001's risk profile to meet the spirit of CPG 220, sector concentration
contexts:
CPS 230 — Prudential Standard: Operational Risk Management — Artificial Intelligence and Automated Decision Systems

APRA has confirmed that the use of artificial intelligence in material operational processes — including credit risk assessment, portfolio monitoring, and regulatory compliance — is subject to CPS 230. An AI system used to assess credit risk, identify regulatory breaches, or produce risk recommendations must meet the following standards: (a) its outputs must be explainable and auditable; (b) every material recommendation must be accompanied by an evidence trail linking the recommendation to underlying data; (c) the system must not make autonomous decisions that would normally require human authorisation; and (d) human oversight must be embedded as an architectural requirement, not merely an optional review step.

CPS 230 — Prudential Standard: Operational Risk Management — Scope and Purpose

CPS 230 establishes APRA's requirements for the management of operational risk by all APRA-regulated entities. The standard applies to all material operational processes, including those involving automated systems, artificial intelligence, and machine learning models. Operational resilience requires that entities maintain the capacity to deliver critical operations during periods of stress and that all material risk decisions are subject to appropriate human oversight. CPS 230 came into full effect on 1 July 2025 and supersedes earlier operational risk guidance that did not explicitly address AI and automated decision systems.

Internal Credit Policy — §7.3 Sector Concentration Limits and Escalation — Sector Concentration — Regulatory Reporting Obligations

Sector concentration monitoring is required not only by internal credit policy but also by APRA's Prudential Practice Guide CPG 220 on credit risk management. Where APRA requests evidence of sector concentration monitoring, the ADI must be able to produce: the sector concentration limits approved by the Credit Risk Committee, current utilisation reports by sector, the escalation history for any sector that has exceeded 75% utilisation, and evidence that connected borrower aggregation has been applied correctly. An ADI that monitors sector concentration at the individual borrower level without aggregating connected party exposures is not meeting the spirit of CPG 220 sector monitoring requirements. The internal sector concentration system must capture SECTOR_CODE from BCA_SECTOR records and aggregate across all connected parties identified through BUT050 and BCA_GUARANTOR relationship mapping.

APS 112 — Prudential Standard: Capital Adequacy — Standardised Approach to Credit Risk — Sector Concentration Risk Capital Add-on

APRA may require ADIs to hold additional capital against portfolios exhibiting material sector concentration. Where an ADI's portfolio has more than 75% of sector exposure in a single sector occupied by connected borrowers sharing common guarantors, the concentration risk is qualitatively higher than a dispersed sector exposure. The capital add-on for sector concentration is determined through the Pillar 2 supervisory review process and is based on the ADI's demonstrated ability to monitor, manage, and stress-test sector concentrations. An ADI that cannot demonstrate connected party aggregation in its sector concentration monitoring may face a Pillar 2 capital requirement in addition to the standard Pillar 1 requirements.

CPS 230 — Prudential Standard: Operational Risk Management — Audit Trail Requirements for AI Decisions

Every material AI-generated recommendation must be supported by a complete audit trail that enables post-hoc review of the reasoning process. The audit trail must include: (a) the specific data records consulted by the AI system; (b) the reasoning steps taken by the AI to reach its recommendation; (c) the confidence level assigned to each finding; (d) any data gaps or uncertainties acknowledged by the system; (e) the identity of the human who reviewed and approved the recommendation; and (f) the timestamp of both the AI recommendation and the human approval. An AI system that generates findings without citing specific TRBK table records as evidence does not meet the audit trail requirement of CPS 230. APRA examiners will assess AI audit trail completeness as part of operational resilience reviews from 2026.

CPS230 — Scope and Purpose
CREDIT_POLICY — Sector Concentration — Regulatory Reporting Obligations
APS112 — Sector Concentration Risk Capital Add-on
CPS230 — Audit Trail Requirements for AI Decisions
ground_truth: APS 221 applies to the connected group large exposure of 92% utilisation. The February 2026 DTI Limit Activation Notice applies to the DTI breach of 7.2 against a limit of 6.0. CPS 230 applies because AI risk intelligence systems used in this assessment must have human oversight before finalising recommendations.

question: What action does APS 221 require when a connected group exposure reaches 92% utilisation of the large exposure limit?
answer:
## APS 221 Requirements at 92% Utilisation of the Large Exposure Limit

Based on the regulatory sources provided, a **92% utilisation** of the large exposure limit triggers obligations at **two threshold levels simultaneously**.

---

### 1. Board Notification Requirement (90% Threshold — Triggered)

Under **Source 1 (APS 221 — Monitoring Thresholds and Board Notification)**, since 92% **exceeds the 90% threshold**, the ADI's Board **must be formally notified within three business days**.

The notification must include all of the following elements:
- The **identity of the connected group**
- The **total exposure amount**
- The **applicable limit**
- The **utilisation percentage** (i.e., 92%)
- A **proposed management action plan**

> ⚠️ Failure to notify the Board at the 90% threshold **constitutes a material compliance deficiency** under APS 221.

---

### 2. Risk Committee Reporting (75% Threshold — Also Triggered)

Since 92% also exceeds the **75% threshold**, the matter must have been (or must be) **reported to the ADI's Risk Committee within five business days** of reaching that earlier threshold (Source 1).

---

### 3. No Breach Yet — But Proximity to Limit

At 92% utilisation, the exposure has **not yet breached** the large exposure limit (which occurs at **100% or above** per Source 2). However, the ADI should note that the large exposure limit itself is **25% of Tier 1 capital** (Sources 3 and 4), and proximity to that limit warrants close monitoring.

---

### Summary Table

| Threshold |
contexts:
APS 221 — Prudential Standard: Large Exposures — Monitoring Thresholds and Board Notification

An ADI must implement a tiered monitoring framework for large exposures. Where the aggregate exposure to a connected group reaches 75% of the applicable large exposure limit, the matter must be reported to the ADI's Risk Committee within five business days. Where the aggregate exposure to a connected group reaches or exceeds 90% of the applicable large exposure limit, the ADI's Board must be formally notified within three business days. The notification must include: the identity of the connected group, total exposure amount, applicable limit, utilisation percentage, and a proposed management action plan. Failure to notify the Board at the 90% threshold constitutes a material compliance deficiency under APS 221.

APS 221 — Prudential Standard: Large Exposures — Breach Reporting and Remediation

An APS 221 breach occurs when the exposure to a single counterparty or connected group exceeds 100% of the applicable large exposure limit. An ADI that identifies a breach must: (a) notify APRA within 5 business days of identification; (b) provide a written explanation of how the breach occurred; (c) submit a remediation plan within 15 business days; and (d) implement the remediation plan within the timeline agreed with APRA. A connected group exposure of 129% of the applicable limit represents a significant breach requiring immediate APRA notification and emergency board review. Failure to identify and report a breach in a timely manner attracts additional regulatory enforcement action under the Banking Act 1959.

APS 221 — Prudential Standard: Large Exposures — Large Exposure Limits — Single and Group

The large exposure limit for a single counterparty must not exceed 25% of the ADI's Tier 1 capital at any point in time. For groups of connected counterparties, the same 25% limit applies to the aggregate exposure across all members of the connected group. An ADI may set internal limits below the APRA maximum — internal limits do not alter the APRA reporting and notification obligations. Where an ADI's systems identify that a borrower's guarantor network creates a connected group exposure, both the single counterparty exposure and the total group exposure must be assessed against their respective limits simultaneously.

APS 221 — Prudential Standard: Large Exposures — Definition of Large Exposure

A large exposure is defined as the total aggregate exposure of an Authorised Deposit-taking Institution (ADI) to a single counterparty or group of connected counterparties that equals or exceeds 10% of the ADI's Tier 1 capital. An ADI must not have a large exposure that exceeds 25% of its Tier 1 capital to any single counterparty or group of connected counterparties without prior APRA approval. Large exposure limits apply to the gross credit exposure before the application of credit risk mitigants, including guarantees and collateral, unless those mitigants are explicitly recognised under APS 221.

APS 221 — Prudential Standard: Large Exposures — Guarantor Exposure Aggregation

Where a guarantor guarantees the obligations of multiple borrowers to the ADI, the total exposure to all guaranteed borrowers must be attributed to the guarantor for large exposure calculation purposes. The combined exposure of all borrowers guaranteed by the same guarantor, together with any direct exposure to the guarantor, constitutes the group exposure for APS 221 purposes. This aggregation applies regardless of whether each individual borrower falls within the single counterparty limit. An ADI that identifies a common guarantor covering multiple borrowers must immediately calculate and monitor the total group exposure against the large exposure limit.
context_titles: APS221 — Monitoring Thresholds and Board Notification
APS221 — Breach Reporting and Remediation
APS221 — Large Exposure Limits — Single and Group
APS221 — Definition of Large Exposure
APS221 — Guarantor Exposure Aggregation
ground_truth: APS 221 requires formal Board notification within three business days when aggregate exposure to a connected group reaches or exceeds 90% of the applicable large exposure limit. The Board notification must include the identity of the connected group, total exposure amount, applicable limit, utilisation percentage, and a proposed management action plan.

question: What is the required remediation timeline for an unreported DTI breach under the February 2026 APRA notice?
answer:
## Remediation Timeline for Unreported DTI Breaches

Based on the provided regulatory sources, there are **two distinct but related timelines** that apply to an unreported DTI breach under the February 2026 APRA DTI Limit Activation Notice:

---

### 1. Reporting Obligation (Source 1)
Where a DTI breach is identified after the **1 February 2026** activation date, the ADI must **notify APRA within 5 business days of identification** via ARF 223. The notification must include:
- Number of affected borrowers
- Aggregate loan value
- Distribution of DTI ratios
- Proposed remediation timeline

An ADI that has not reported an identified breach (e.g., a borrower with a DTI ratio of 7.2) is **already in breach of its reporting obligations** and attracts **additional regulatory scrutiny** under the supervisory framework.

---

### 2. Remediation Plan Submission (Source 2)
For a **material breach**, a remediation plan must be **submitted to APRA within 30 business days** of identification. Additionally:
- A **monitoring plan must be implemented within 15 business days** of identification for loans with DTI ratios above 6.0
- For DTI ratios **above 8.0**, the ADI must implement **active remediation**, including review of loan structure and assessment of additional security or guarantor support

---

### Key Consequence of Non-Reporting
Failure to report within 5 business days constitutes a **separate compliance breach** from the underlying DTI breach itself, compounding the ADI's regulatory exposure.
contexts:
APRA DTI Limit Activation Notice — February 2026 — Reporting Requirements — APRA Notification

ADIs must report all identified DTI breaches to APRA within the standard Authorised Deposit-taking Institution Prudential Reporting Form ARF 223. Where a borrower DTI breach is identified after the 1 February 2026 activation date, the ADI must notify APRA within 5 business days of identification. The notification must include: the number of affected borrowers, the aggregate loan value, the distribution of DTI ratios, and the proposed remediation timeline. An ADI that identifies a borrower with a DTI ratio of 7.2 as of the activation date and has not yet reported this to APRA is in breach of its reporting obligations under the February 2026 DTI Limit Activation Notice. Unreported breaches attract additional regulatory scrutiny under the supervisory framework.

APRA DTI Limit Activation Notice — February 2026 — Remediation Requirements and Timeline

Where an ADI identifies existing loans with DTI ratios above 6.0, the ADI must implement a monitoring plan within 15 business days of identification. The monitoring plan must include: quarterly DTI reassessment, income verification at each renewal or variation, and escalation triggers for DTI increases above 8.0. For borrowers with DTI ratios above 8.0 or approaching 9.0 due to income expiry, the ADI must implement active remediation including review of the loan structure and assessment of the need for additional security or guarantor support. APRA may require the ADI to reduce the DTI through additional repayments, loan restructuring, or the application of additional security. Remediation plans must be submitted to APRA within 30 business days of the identification of a material breach.

APRA DTI Limit Activation Notice — February 2026 — Existing Portfolio — Identification and Documentation

ADIs are required to identify all existing loans in their portfolio where the current borrower DTI exceeds 6.0. These pre-existing breaches must be documented within 5 business days of identification. Documentation must include: the borrower's identity, the current DTI ratio, the date the loan was originated, the income basis used at origination, and any changes in income since origination that have affected the DTI. An ADI that identifies a borrower with a DTI of 7.2 must document this within 5 business days and report the identification to APRA through the standard prudential reporting framework. Failure to identify and document existing breaches within the required timeframe is a separate compliance obligation from the reporting of the breach itself.

APS 221 — Prudential Standard: Large Exposures — Breach Reporting and Remediation

An APS 221 breach occurs when the exposure to a single counterparty or connected group exceeds 100% of the applicable large exposure limit. An ADI that identifies a breach must: (a) notify APRA within 5 business days of identification; (b) provide a written explanation of how the breach occurred; (c) submit a remediation plan within 15 business days; and (d) implement the remediation plan within the timeline agreed with APRA. A connected group exposure of 129% of the applicable limit represents a significant breach requiring immediate APRA notification and emergency board review. Failure to identify and report a breach in a timely manner attracts additional regulatory enforcement action under the Banking Act 1959.

APRA DTI Limit Activation Notice — February 2026 — Activation of DTI Limit — Effective Date and Scope

The Australian Prudential Regulation Authority (APRA) hereby activates restrictions on new residential mortgage lending where the borrower's debt-to-income (DTI) ratio exceeds 6.0. This restriction takes effect from 1 February 2026 and applies to all Authorised Deposit-taking Institutions (ADIs) regulated under the Banking Act 1959. The DTI limit of 6.0 applies to all new residential mortgage originations from the activation date. An ADI must not extend new residential mortgage credit to any borrower whose DTI ratio would exceed 6.0 as a result of the new lending.
context_titles: DTI_LIMIT_FEB2026 — Reporting Requirements — APRA Notification
DTI_LIMIT_FEB2026 — Remediation Requirements and Timeline
DTI_LIMIT_FEB2026 — Existing Portfolio — Identification and Documentation
APS221 — Breach Reporting and Remediation
DTI_LIMIT_FEB2026 — Activation of DTI Limit — Effective Date and Scope
ground_truth: Under the February 2026 DTI Limit Activation Notice, identified DTI breaches must be documented within 5 business days of identification and reported to APRA. Remediation plans must be submitted to APRA within 30 business days of identifying a material breach. A monitoring plan must be implemented within 15 business days.

question: Does B-001 risk profile require human approval before AI recommendations are finalised, and which APRA standard requires this?
answer:
## Human Approval Requirement for AI Risk Recommendations

### Direct Answer

Yes, any AI-generated risk recommendation — including one pertaining to a borrower or entity such as B-001 — **requires mandatory human approval before it is finalised or communicated to decision-makers**.

---

### Governing APRA Standard

This requirement is established under **CPS 230 — Prudential Standard: Operational Risk Management**, specifically the **Human Oversight / Co-Pilot Requirement** provisions.

---

### Specific Requirements That Apply

**1. Mandatory Pre-Finalisation Human Checkpoint**
> *"An ADI using AI for credit risk intelligence must implement a mandatory human-in-the-loop checkpoint before any AI-generated risk recommendation is **finalised or communicated to decision-makers**."*
> *(Source 1)*

**2. Post-Hoc Review Is Explicitly Insufficient**
> *"This requirement is not satisfied by a post-hoc review of AI outputs — the human oversight must occur **before** the recommendation is presented as a concluded finding."*
> *(Source 1)*

**3. Architectural Requirement, Not Procedural**
> Human oversight must be *"implemented at the architectural level, not merely as a procedural control."*
> *(Sources 1 & 2)*

**4. No Autonomous Decision-Making**
> The AI system *"must not make autonomous decisions that would normally require human authorisation."*
> *(Source 2)*

---

### Audit Trail Obligation Linked to Approval

Per **Source 3**, the audit trail for any B-001 recommendation must capture:
- **(e)** the **identity of the human** who reviewed and approved the recommendation; and
- **(f)
contexts:
CPS 230 — Prudential Standard: Operational Risk Management — Human Oversight — The Co-Pilot Requirement

APRA's position on AI in banking is explicit: AI can be a valuable co-pilot, but it must never be an autopilot. An ADI using AI for credit risk intelligence must implement a mandatory human-in-the-loop checkpoint before any AI-generated risk recommendation is finalised or communicated to decision-makers. This requirement is not satisfied by a post-hoc review of AI outputs — the human oversight must occur before the recommendation is presented as a concluded finding. An AI risk intelligence system that generates a risk brief without a human approval step is not compliant with CPS 230, regardless of the accuracy of its findings. The human-in-the-loop checkpoint must be implemented at the architectural level, not merely as a procedural control.

CPS 230 — Prudential Standard: Operational Risk Management — Artificial Intelligence and Automated Decision Systems

APRA has confirmed that the use of artificial intelligence in material operational processes — including credit risk assessment, portfolio monitoring, and regulatory compliance — is subject to CPS 230. An AI system used to assess credit risk, identify regulatory breaches, or produce risk recommendations must meet the following standards: (a) its outputs must be explainable and auditable; (b) every material recommendation must be accompanied by an evidence trail linking the recommendation to underlying data; (c) the system must not make autonomous decisions that would normally require human authorisation; and (d) human oversight must be embedded as an architectural requirement, not merely an optional review step.

CPS 230 — Prudential Standard: Operational Risk Management — Audit Trail Requirements for AI Decisions

Every material AI-generated recommendation must be supported by a complete audit trail that enables post-hoc review of the reasoning process. The audit trail must include: (a) the specific data records consulted by the AI system; (b) the reasoning steps taken by the AI to reach its recommendation; (c) the confidence level assigned to each finding; (d) any data gaps or uncertainties acknowledged by the system; (e) the identity of the human who reviewed and approved the recommendation; and (f) the timestamp of both the AI recommendation and the human approval. An AI system that generates findings without citing specific TRBK table records as evidence does not meet the audit trail requirement of CPS 230. APRA examiners will assess AI audit trail completeness as part of operational resilience reviews from 2026.

CPS 230 — Prudential Standard: Operational Risk Management — Board and Senior Management Accountability

The Board of an ADI is responsible for ensuring that AI systems used in material operational processes are transparent, auditable, and subject to appropriate human governance. Senior management must approve the use of any AI system in credit risk assessment and must receive regular reporting on AI system performance, including accuracy, confidence levels, and any instances where AI confidence fell below acceptable thresholds. The Board must be notified of any AI system output that results in a regulatory breach finding or a recommendation to report to APRA. AI system governance must be included in the ADI's operational risk management framework and reviewed annually. A Board that has not explicitly considered and approved AI use in credit risk assessment is not compliant with CPS 230.

CPS 230 — Prudential Standard: Operational Risk Management — Scope and Purpose

CPS 230 establishes APRA's requirements for the management of operational risk by all APRA-regulated entities. The standard applies to all material operational processes, including those involving automated systems, artificial intelligence, and machine learning models. Operational resilience requires that entities maintain the capacity to deliver critical operations during periods of stress and that all material risk decisions are subject to appropriate human oversight. CPS 230 came into full effect on 1 July 2025 and supersedes earlier operational risk guidance that did not explicitly address AI and automated decision systems.
context_titles: CPS230 — Human Oversight — The Co-Pilot Requirement
CPS230 — Artificial Intelligence and Automated Decision Systems
CPS230 — Audit Trail Requirements for AI Decisions
CPS230 — Board and Senior Management Accountability
CPS230 — Scope and Purpose
ground_truth: Yes. CPS 230 requires that all material AI-generated risk recommendations be subject to mandatory human review before finalisation. CPS 230 states that AI can be a co-pilot but must never be an autopilot. The human-in-the-loop checkpoint must be implemented at the architectural level and is not optional.

question: What defines a connected group of counterparties under APS 221, and how does a family trust structure qualify?
answer:
## Connected Counterparty Groups Under APS 221

### Definition of a Connected Group

Under APS 221, a group of connected counterparties consists of **two or more natural persons or legal entities linked in such a way that financial distress in one would likely cause financial distress in another** [Source 1].

Entities are considered connected where any of the following conditions exist:

| Criterion | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| **(a)** | One entity **controls** another |
| **(b)** | Entities are under **common ownership or common management** |
| **(c)** | A **family trust structure** exists where beneficiaries are counterparties of the ADI |
| **(d)** | One entity **guarantees the obligations** of another entity to the ADI |
| **(e)** | Entities **share key personnel or board members** in a manner that could result in coordinated financial distress |

Critically, **the substance of the relationship governs, not its legal form** [Source 1].

---

### How Family Trust Structures Qualify

Family trust structures qualify as a connected group through two reinforcing mechanisms:

1. **Direct Criterion**: As explicitly listed in criterion (c) above, a family trust structure where beneficiaries are counterparties of the ADI constitutes a connection [Source 1].

2. **Aggregation Obligation**: Where two or more borrowers share a common family trust structure — **whether as trustee, beneficiary, or guarantor** — their combined exposure must be treated as a connected group for APS 221 purposes [Source 2].

APRA specifically recognises that family trust structures **"present particular concentration risk because beneficial interests are often not disclosed in standard loan applications"** [Source 2],
contexts:
APS 221 — Prudential Standard: Large Exposures — Connected Counterparties — Definition

A group of connected counterparties consists of two or more natural persons or legal entities that are linked in such a way that financial distress in one would likely cause financial distress in another. Entities are considered connected where: (a) one entity controls another; (b) entities are under common ownership or common management; (c) a family trust structure exists where beneficiaries are counterparties of the ADI; (d) one entity guarantees the obligations of another entity to the ADI; or (e) entities share key personnel or board members in a manner that could result in coordinated financial distress. The substance of the relationship governs, not its legal form.

APS 221 — Prudential Standard: Large Exposures — Family Trust Structures

Family trust structures present particular concentration risk because beneficial interests are often not disclosed in standard loan applications. Where an ADI identifies that two or more borrowers share a common family trust structure — as trustee, beneficiary, or guarantor — their combined exposure must be treated as a connected group for APS 221 purposes. The relationship type FAMILY_TRUST_MEMBER in SAP TRBK BUT050 relationship records captures such connections. An ADI that identifies multiple borrowers linked through a family trust must aggregate all guarantor-supported exposures within that trust structure when assessing APS 221 compliance.

APS 221 — Prudential Standard: Large Exposures — Six-Hop Relationship Traversal Requirement

APRA expects ADIs to implement systems capable of identifying connected counterparty relationships that extend beyond immediate contractual links. Connected party relationships can span multiple degrees of separation: a borrower's guarantor may be linked through trust structures to additional guarantors who support other borrowers. An ADI relying solely on individual loan assessments without traversing the full relationship network is not compliant with APS 221 connected counterparty identification requirements. Systems must be capable of traversing at least six degrees of relationship to identify all members of a connected counterparty group and aggregate their exposures accurately.

APS 112 — Prudential Standard: Capital Adequacy — Standardised Approach to Credit Risk — Credit Risk Capital — Connected Party Classification

Under APS 112, an ADI must assign appropriate risk weights to all credit exposures and calculate the required capital to hold against those exposures. Where a borrower is part of a connected group for APS 221 purposes, the ADI must also consider whether the connected party relationship affects the credit risk assessment under APS 112. Specifically, where a guarantor's financial stress would result in simultaneous deterioration across multiple guaranteed exposures, the ADI must assess whether the correlation of default risk within the connected group requires additional capital beyond what individual borrower risk weights would suggest. Connected party relationships identified through BUT050 and BCA_GUARANTOR relationship records are material inputs to APS 112 capital adequacy calculations.

Internal Credit Policy — §7.3 Sector Concentration Limits and Escalation — Connected Borrower Aggregation for Sector Monitoring

Where multiple borrowers are connected through common guarantors, shared ownership structures, or family trust arrangements, and all fall within the same industry sector, their combined exposure must be aggregated for sector concentration monitoring purposes. A guarantor network connecting four borrowers — B-001, B-002, B-003, and B-007 — all of whom have SECTOR_CODE = RETAIL_PROP, creates a systemic sector concentration that exceeds what individual loan analysis reveals. The systemic risk of four connected borrowers in the same sector, exposed through the same guarantor network, is qualitatively different from four unconnected borrowers in the same sector and must be escalated separately even if the sector limit has not been formally reached. Credit Policy §7.3.3 requires that any connected guarantor network spanning four or more borrowers in the same sector is reported to the Risk Committee regardless of utilisation percentage.
context_titles: APS221 — Connected Counterparties — Definition
APS221 — Family Trust Structures
APS221 — Six-Hop Relationship Traversal Requirement
APS112 — Credit Risk Capital — Connected Party Classification
CREDIT_POLICY — Connected Borrower Aggregation for Sector Monitoring
ground_truth: Under APS 221, a group of connected counterparties exists where entities are linked such that financial distress in one would likely cause distress in another. A family trust structure qualifies as a connected group where beneficiaries are counterparties of the ADI. Entities linked through a common family trust structure must have their combined exposures aggregated as a single connected group for APS 221 large exposure limit purposes.
